sentiment.rationale: A call to repentance is followed by comforting visions of horses patrolling a peaceful earth and God's jealousy for Jerusalem's restoration.
A vision of Jerusalem expanding beyond walls; God promises to dwell in her midst and many nations shall join themselves to the Lord.
Joshua the high priest stands before the angel, has his filthy garments replaced with clean ones, and the Branch is prophesied.
The vision of the golden candlestick and two olive trees; Zerubbabel shall finish the temple not by might but by the Spirit.
Two visions of judgment: a flying roll (scroll) of curses upon thieves and swearers, and wickedness carried away in an ephah to Shinar.
Four chariots go forth as divine agents; Joshua is crowned, prefiguring the Branch who shall build the temple and be both priest and king.
A delegation asks about fasting; God responds that He desires true justice and mercy rather than ritual mourning, recalling the disobedience of the fathers.
Ten promises of restoration: old men and women in the streets, children playing, truth and peace, and nations seeking the Lord in Jerusalem.
Judgment upon neighboring nations is followed by the Messianic king riding into Jerusalem on a donkey, bringing salvation and peace.
God promises to strengthen Judah and save Ephraim, gathering His scattered people and making them mighty as they walk in His name.
The good shepherd is rejected and valued at thirty pieces of silver; the foolish shepherd takes his place, bringing judgment upon the flock.
Jerusalem becomes a burdensome stone; God pours out the spirit of grace and supplication, and they mourn for the one whom they pierced.
A fountain is opened for sin; false prophets are ashamed; the shepherd is smitten and the sheep scattered; a refined remnant survives.
The day of the Lord: Jerusalem is besieged, the Lord stands on the Mount of Olives, living waters flow, and the Lord becomes king over all the earth.

missingCharacters.name: Zechariah (prophet)
missingCharacters.description: A post-exilic prophet and priest who received eight night visions and Messianic prophecies during the rebuilding of the Second Temple.

summaries.summary: After a call to repentance, Zechariah sees visions of horsemen patrolling a peaceful earth and four horns with four carpenters who will restore Judah.
A man with a measuring line is told Jerusalem will be inhabited without walls because God Himself will be a wall of fire around her and dwell in her midst.
Joshua the high priest, accused by Satan, is cleansed by the angel and given clean garments; the coming of the Branch is prophesied.
The vision of the golden candlestick fed by two olive trees; Zerubbabel's hands shall finish the temple — not by might nor power, but by God's Spirit.
Two visions of judgment: a flying scroll of curses against sin, and a woman called Wickedness carried in an ephah to the land of Shinar.
Four chariots go forth as agents of God's judgment; Joshua the high priest is crowned, symbolizing the Branch who shall build the temple of the Lord.
When asked about continuing to fast, God declares He desires justice, mercy, and compassion rather than empty ritual, recalling the disobedience that led to exile.
God promises to return to Zion with ten blessings: restored streets, prosperous fields, truth and peace, and the nations seeking the Lord in Jerusalem.
Judgment falls upon Syria, Tyre, and Philistia; Zion's king comes riding on a donkey, just and having salvation; prisoners of hope are freed.
God promises rain, rebukes false shepherds, and pledges to gather Ephraim and Judah from exile, strengthening them to walk in His name.
The good shepherd tends the doomed flock but is rejected; he is valued at thirty pieces of silver, cast to the potter; a foolish shepherd arises.
Jerusalem becomes a burdensome stone for all peoples; God defends Judah; the spirit of grace and supplication is poured out, and they mourn for the one they pierced.
A fountain is opened for sin; false prophets will be ashamed of their visions; the shepherd is smitten, the sheep scattered, and a refined third remains.
All nations gather against Jerusalem; the Lord descends upon the Mount of Olives, which cleaves in two; living waters flow and the Lord reigns as king over all the earth.

crossReferences.note: The Branch — a messianic title connecting to Isaiah's prophecy of a shoot from the stem of Jesse.
'Not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit' — a principle echoed in Restoration scripture.
Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey, fulfilling this prophecy; Matthew quotes it directly.
Thirty pieces of silver — the price for which Judas betrayed Jesus.
The thirty pieces cast to the potter — Matthew attributes this to Jeremiah but draws from Zechariah.
'They shall look upon me whom they have pierced' — applied by John to Christ on the cross.
'What are these wounds in thine hands?' — fulfilled at the Second Coming when the Jews recognize Christ.
Jesus quotes this at Gethsemane: 'I will smite the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock shall be scattered abroad.'
The Mount of Olives — the place of Christ's ascension and prophesied return.
Living waters flowing from Jerusalem — parallels Ezekiel's vision of the temple river.

verses.reason: 'Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion: for, lo, I come, and I will dwell in the midst of thee.'
Introduction of 'my servant the BRANCH' — a key Messianic title pointing to Christ.
'Not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the Lord of hosts' — one of the most quoted verses in all of scripture.
'Execute true judgment, and shew mercy and compassions every man to his brother' — the essence of covenant living.
'Ten men... shall take hold of the skirt of him that is a Jew' — a prophecy of the nations seeking Israel's God.
'Thy King cometh unto thee... lowly, and riding upon an ass' — fulfilled at Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem.
'They weighed for my price thirty pieces of silver' — the price of a slave, fulfilled in Judas's betrayal of Christ.
'They shall look upon me whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn' — a powerful prophecy of Christ's crucifixion and future recognition.
'What are these wounds in thine hands?' — LDS doctrine connects this to Christ's appearance at the Second Coming.
'Smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered' — quoted by Jesus before Gethsemane.
'His feet shall stand in that day upon the mount of Olives, and the mount of Olives shall cleave in the midst' — a key Second Coming prophecy.
'The Lord shall be king over all the earth: in that day shall there be one Lord, and his name one.'
